course_description: Economics focuses on choices between alternative ways of using resources and the consequences of these choices, which often have to be made in the face of problems associated with the availability of information and limitations to knowledge. These choices concern issues such as what to consume, what to produce, how to produce it, how to organize production between different economic entities, and what prices to charge. Choices taken by individuals need to be coordinated to avoid wasted opportunities, the more so the more that specialization is taking place at the level of the individual, firm or national economy. A major coordinating role is played by price mechanisms, i.e. adjustments in relative prices and the incentives provided by profits and losses. However, much coordination is achieved via the use of planning and institutions. Even with coordination assisted by these devices coordination failures can still occur, such as major slumps in employment and international trade. Economic theory can help to assess when these problems are likely and how they may be dealt with.

requirements.incompatible: ECON2010, or 2400 or 7003 or 7010 or 7011 or 7110 or MEBS7103
requirements.assumed_background: No background in economics is assumed. However, if you want to do some reading before the course starts so as to get a feel for the economic way of thinking, then a good source for microeconomics would be Tim Hardford (2006) The Undercover Economist , Oxford, Oxford University Press. For macroeconomics, perhaps try Michael Stewart, Keynes and After (various editions), Harmondsworth, Penguin. Reading the articles in The Economist is also a good way of gaining insight into some contempory economic issues.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
This course aims to enable you to:

Develop the ability to link economic theory with everyday issues so as to encourage critical, independent thinking to promote alternative views and new solutions.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify situations where economic principles and theories are applicable to and directly assist in business or organisational decision making.
Apply economic theory to business problems in an informed and analytical way.
Critically evaluate choices made by individuals and firms using economic concepts.
Employ economic theory and analysis to create solutions for strategic issues faced by individuals or businesses.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
Group Presentation.

Students are required to form small groups of four to five students and this will depend on final enrolment numbers. The presentation by the group is to be 15 minutes, followed by 10 minutes discussion and class feedback. Time limits will be strictly applied. The topic, date and time for each group presentation will be allocated during the first tutorial.

Each group will be allocated a company nominated by the course co-ordinator. All companies will be listed on the Australian Stock Exchange. Students are expected to access information from company websites, the ASX website, news articles, or other similarly publically available sources to conduct any analysis. Students should not contact or approach any company directly for information. The specific requirements of the group presentation are summarised in the “Group Presentation Marking Guide” (see attached link below, as well as posted on Blackboard). Students are expected to fully address each of the specific requirements of the Group Presentation which are:

1. Using economic theory, analyse and explain past company performances . A brief overview of the company will be needed to set the scene. You must assess whether or not previous management strategic choices can be justified using economic theory. Based on your historical analysis, clearly identify and outline the lessons your group believes the company should learn, or has learnt, that would be beneficial to the company.

2. Having considered the past, examine the present and future direction of the company. You should conduct both an internal and external environmental analysis of the company using analytical tools such as SWOT, PESTEL, Porter’s Five Forces, etc. The details of any analysis are NOT to be included in the presentation. However, the group is required to very briefly summarise the main findings of your environmental analysis to paint a clear picture of the environment the company currently operates in. This should result in your group being able to justify, and clearly state, the key strategic issue the company currently faces.

3. Based on past performances, the lessons learnt, current environment and the company’s position, propose one option to overcome your identified key strategic issue . Fully justify why this option will work using economic theory. You need to also show how it can be implemented by the company (time lines, available resources, etc.) and that it will work in practice.

After your presentation, the group must then have at least one question prepared to promote class discussion and/or debate. The group (or nominated member) is required to lead this class discussion for 10 minutes. The discussion should aim to expand on the theoretical concepts explored in the presentation. The class will finally be expected to provide feedback to the group on whether they believe the proposed option makes sense and if they think it will work.

At the end of the discussion, a nominated “marking group” will be required to give approximately 3 minutes of constructive feedback to the presenting group based on the marking rubric. This marking group will then award a mark, guided by the marking rubric and filling out the Group Presentation Marking Guide. The marking group’s mark will contribute a maximum of 5 out of 15 for the presentation. The lecturer’s mark will contribute a maximum of 10 out of 15 marks for the presentation. The combined scaled marks will give a total final mark out of 15.

assessment_details.title: Individual Newspaper Article Reviews
assessment_details.task_description:
Review two newspaper articles (20% each)

By referring to any Australian or International newspapers, find two articles that relates to at least one microeconomic concept discussed in the first week of class. This newspaper articles must be written in English, can be scanned or obtained from online sources, and published after 1 January, 2017 . Radio and TV transcripts are NOT to be used. Your newspaper articles must satisfy these requirements to pass this assessment task. The two articles should be unrelated and address fundamentally different topics.

Using no more than 1000 words , complete the following.

Briefly summarise the article in a sentence or two, listing the economic concepts you will need to refer to in this assignment.

Explain/support the article’s view by clearly stating any assumptions it makes and applying economic concepts.

By using alternative assumptions and economic concepts, critically analyse the article and present a different perspective.

Summarise one economic lesson learnt from your analysis. Then, by naming some “other actual company” (in a completely different industry used in the article), discuss how the lesson learnt would be relevant. Then, as a manager of this “other actual company”, explain one recommendation you would implement based on this lesson learnt.

Self-assess your assignment by filling out each section of the Newspaper Article Marking Criteria Sheet, being sure to give an overall mark for your assignment. Also complete the section “List specific feedback requests” provided on this Marking Criteria Sheet.

Please ensure you honestly self-assess. If your self-assessment is wildly inaccurate or you have not given a mark for each section and the total mark, your assignment will be returned unmarked. You will be asked to complete the self-assessment and resubmit within two days. After these two days, the usual late penalty will apply. This self-assessment will not influence the grade awarded for this piece of assessment. However, your self-assessment thoughts are intended to be incorporated into the marker’s feedback. Please ensure your completed self-assessment marking criteria sheet is attached as the last page of your assignment . The word document Marking Criteria Sheet that you must fill in and attach at the end of your assignment is available on Blackboard.

IMPORTANT : Please ensure that each newspaper article submission is compiled as follows:

First page is a cover sheet with your name and student number.

Second page is the relevant news article.

Your written work.

Final page shows your self-assessed mark by filling in the Marking Criteria sheet provided. You should indicate areas where feedback is required.

While both article reviews are due on the 8th of July (just before the second week of class), students are encouraged to submit the first article early in order to benefit from feedback. The article reviews should be submitted via Turnitin (blackboard), and make sure that the two article reviews are submitted separately.

assessment_details.title: Individual Essay
assessment_details.task_description:
Major Assignment .

Write an essay on how the economic theories and concepts from this subject impact your organisation (where you are currently working or have worked in the past). The essay should not exceed 2500 words (including the executive summary), and must address the following points:

An Executive Summary is to be provided ( included in the assignment word count).

Background information on the student’s current (or previous) workplace firm or organisation is to be briefly outlined.

Using economic theory (incorporating graphs and relevant calculations), critically analyse past performances of your firm in relation to the market it operated in. Similarly, critically analyse the current performance of your firm in relation to the current market environment in which it now operates.

Based on the critical analysis, a detailed reflection on lessons learned , what was confirmed, and what remains puzzling about your organisation and the market it operates in. Clear linkages should be evident from your analysis and reflection to reveal what you perceive as the key strategic issue your firm faces either now or in the future. It is advisable to use additional relevant analytical tools (e.g. SWOT, PESTEL, Porter’s Five Force analysis) to provide insights to the economic analysis of your firm’s past, present and future performances. This should also help you identify the key strategic issue your firm faces. Conclusions from non-economic analysitical tools should be included in the assignment, but the details should only be included in an Appendix ( not counted towards word limit).

Based on the lesson(s) you learnt from your critical analysis, and combining this with your identified key strategic issue, you must list two clear options to address the key strategic issue. Both options need to be able to be implemented. For each option, use economic theory to fully evaluate how each option addresses the strategic issue and highlight the benefits to the firm of implementing each option. Be sure to discuss and assess the assumptions you will have made when evaluating each option, and look at the impacts on your firm for each option if your assumptions prove to be incorrect.

Draws a conclusion from your analysis. Provides a clearly supported recommendation(s) for the management to address the identified strategic issue, along with implementation guidelines.

Self-assess your assignment using the Major Assignment Marking Criteria word document that is available on Blackboard. If your self-assessment is wildly inaccurate, your assignment will be returned unmarked. You will be asked to complete the self-assessment and resubmit within two days. After these two days, the usual late penalty will apply. This self-assessment will not influence the grade awarded for this piece of assessment. However, your self-assessment thoughts are intended to be incorporated into the marker’s feedback. Please ensure your completed self-assessment Major Assignment Marking Criteria sheet is attached as the last page of your assignment.

When submitting your assignment, please check:

the first page is a cover page (with your name and student number), followed by

your written assignment, then

the marking criteria sheet with your self-assessment and mark.

Submit your assignment online via Blackboard by the due date. To do this, go to the “Assessment” tab on Blackboard and follow the instructions there. Please note that you have to submit the assignment through both Blackboard AND Turnitin by the due date.

learning_activities.topic: 1. Economic fundamentals (Lecture): Key economic concepts such as opportunity cost, marginal analysis, economic models, comparative advantage, price mechanism, and market institutions. Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 1 & 2)
2. Price theory - demand & supply (Lecture): Demand curves, supply curves, equilibrium Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapter 3)
3. Price theory - elasticities (Lecture): Responding to incentives, calculating elasticities, linking price and revenue Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapter 4)
4. Price theory - efficiency (Lecture): Measuring economic welfare, gains from trade, price controls, economics of tax Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapter 5)
5. Producer theory - basics (Lecture): Factors of production, cost curves, perfect competition Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 6 & 7)
6. Producer theory - monopoly (Lecture): Monopoly power, anti-trust policy, price setting Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapter 8)
7. Producer theory - strategic behaviour (Lecture): Game theory, product differentiation, expectations & reputations Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapter 9)
8. Microeconomic policy (Lecture): Market failures, government failures, public policy analysis Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 10 & 11)
9. Introduction to Macroeconomics (Lecture): GDP, economic growth, employment, inflation, business cycles Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 12-14)
10. Monetary economics (Lecture): Money & credit, financial institutions, bubbles & busts Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 15)
11. Macroeconomic policy (Lecture): Monetary policy, fiscal policy, balance of payments Readings/Ref: Hubbard et al. (Chapters 16 & 17)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/documents/studentguides/guidforassignpres.pdf.

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers a range of economic theories and concepts of direct relevance and application to the business environment. The course assumes little or no formal training in economics, notwithstanding the practical economic problems that businesses and consumers confront daily. It aims to provide a solid grounding in the way economists think, the core theories of contemporary economics and how this is relates to the business environment and business strategy. Emphasis is placed upon the application of economic theory rather than the theory itself in a manner reminiscent of the business economists of the USA who form an integral part of American corporate strategy. This theme is developed not only in lectures, but also through student activities including reviewing current newspaper articles, in-class case presentations, and writing a reflective essay on their current work organization and experience. The course includes both micro economics (a study of the economics of consumers, firms, individual industries and Government agencies) and macroeconomics (the study of the domestic and international economies as whole and the environment that firms and other economic agents face externally and over which they exercise little control). The early lectures cover microeconomic concepts with an emphasis on market (demand and supply) conditions, price setting, profit and revenue and cost considerations and differing market structures. Later, problems in allocating available resources with limited information and knowledge are discussed. The linkages between economic concepts and business disciplines such as marketing, finance, and human resource management will become apparent through the course. The lectures in the latter part of the course stress the macro element and are designed to examine the way in which the wider economic environment within Australia and overseas influences the micro decisions of economic agents. Concepts such as Government policy (including taxation and monetary policy) shifts in exchange rates, Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and Gross National Expenditure (GNE), inflation, unemployment and aggregate demand and supply, monetary and fiscal policy and their relevance to business decision making will be analysed. At the end of the course the student will be able to critically evaluate current economic conditions (for example, through articles in the Australian Financial Review and The Economist) and determine their business relevance to his or her organisation.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

No feedback available at this time. We look forward to receiving your feedback at the end of the semester.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0 - 19.99%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20 - 44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

45 - 49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50 - 64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65 - 74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75 - 84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85+
late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made online via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. Extensions cannot exceed the number of days you suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate.

Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment may be accessed through: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/extension-progressive-assessment .

Assessment submitted after the due date without prior approval will entail a penalty of 5% of the total mark available for the assessment item for each calendar day after the due date.

Assessment submitted after three days from the due date will receive no marks.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise specified, ALL assignments must be lodged by the due date and time stated in the course profile, and are to be submitted through both Blackboard and Turnitin. The following should also be noted.

1. Students must pay attention to the order in which pages of an assignment are to be collated. Requirements for assignment submissions are under section 5.5 "Assessment Details".

2. Students will have acknowledged the plagiarism Statute of the University via their submission through Turnitin and must keep a copy of their submitted work.

3. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details.

Please also note:

· Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement.

· Incomplete or incorrect assignment coversheets can cause delays in processing your assignments.
